item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in the article could not be sourced. Thus, it is to be assumed that they're non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a couple of issues with this one. The first one is that this really doesn't seem like anomalous in nature, the incidents could have easily been coincidental and with no foundation testing at all to confirm if it's anomalous in nature I don't see why they'd give it any sort of classification.

Secondly, which it seems like a couple of others seem to also take issue with, is that the document seems to be badly written in the way of explaining what is exactly anomalous about the site. There's an incredible excess of information that doesn't add to the explanation of the scp, and makes it read to me as repeating itself.

I think my main issue with this one overall is the lack of foundation testing to confirm any anomalous nature.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer expired - edits implemented.
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Proposed Edits
- Proposed Edits

The edit proposed is to link the article to the Site-81 dossier page. The article mentions the site in its containment procedures, and the dossier also mentions the article.

Editing this:

Containment is to be managed from Site 81.

To this:

Containment is to be managed from Site-81.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In incident d, the first sentence of the of the block uses as instead of at.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Spoilers
- I warned you

I like the imperfections of this. It could be a perfect justice-dispensing anomaly, but frankly the world of fiction has tons of those; this version is more interesting for its scarcity. Also, letting it make mistakes and respond appropriately gives it character and gives the reader more emotional connection.

The last part of Incident D was not clear from the article, or even the first hint. I get that the death date is supposed to be a hint, but since every other apparent criminal in the article ends up dead, I didn't actually question E's death story at all- I figured "that's a minimum-security facility" meant "he died of some anomalous happenstance", not "he isn't actually dead."

The mistake of Incident B is particularly interesting characterization. I had been thinking "oh shit, his partner shot him, corruption or something," so hearing the anomaly's even simpler concept of the situation threw me. It didn't think anything a human might think about a cop with a gunshot wound, even about dissension in the ranks- it just went after the visiting guy who had a gun. That makes it pretty alien, in retrospect- and I like it being alien, even while it cares about people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. I'm glad you liked the explanation for incident report a.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have added a sentence to the description, does that address your concerns?

item.title: Spoilers
item.content:encoded:
Ok, it has been long enough. Here is an explanation of exactly what went down in each incident:

+ (spoiler) General
– hide block

Pretty much a classic haunted building; in this case the hospital mainly attempts to take revenge on the people it believes are responsible for its patient's injuries. Unfortunately, it isn't always correct in determining who was responsible, and in some cases its judgement is affected by the perceptions of the people in it, and it jumps to incorrect conclusions with unfortunate results in incidents a, b and f.

+ (spoiler) Incident Report 2564-a
– hide block

The first part of the hospital to become anomalous was the psychiatric wing, due to POI-58436. SCP-2564 then does some anomalous stuff due to the altered perceptions of its occupants as to the cause of their illnesses. There really isn't a lot to say here other than 'random anomalous stuff happened'.

+ (spoiler) Incident Report 2564-b
– hide block

Officer Walker was shot by Timayev, however the hospital mistakenly believed that Dunn had shot Walker, since he was carrying a weapon that can also cause similar injuries.

+ (spoiler) Incident Report 2564-c
– hide block

Kyle Law was the one to break Richard Law's arm. The hospital took him out because of that.

+ (spoiler) Incident Report 2564-d
– hide block

Ethan Warren had intended to kill Reese Warren when he came to the hospital. The hospital did something that caused him to remit his previously criminal ways. He did not actually die in prison; rather he agreed to testify against the criminal organization he had previously been part of and was moved to a witness protection program.

+ (spoiler) Incident Report 2564-e
– hide block

It should be fairly obvious what happened here: Carl Hazen was driving drunk, hit a tree, and killed or nearly killed all of his passengers. The hospital got angry and killed him.

+ (spoiler) Incident Report 2564-f
– hide block

Mr. Dean had poisoned Mr. Moyer with antifreeze, but the hospital thought that Mrs. Moyer had done it, and killed Mrs. Moyer. Then when Mr. Dean then showed up to finish the job, the hospital realized its mistake and kills Mr. Dean, later putting up the plaque.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

You are correct in that the hospital thought Dunn had shot Walker. However, police forces in Indiana used the Glock 17 9mm during this period. (Note that I have updated things to make it clearer that the bullet could not have been from Dunn's weapon.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So this was long and confusing, and didn’t really come across as anomalous for me, echoing what TL333s said. It just came across as a hospital where bad things happen, which is a poor result to achieve given the length and dryness of the incident logs.

-1. This was too long and had too little pay off for my taste.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would be perfectly acceptable to say "Persons associated with patients accepted to the hospital are statistically more likely to undergo a harmful or fatal event" yadda yadda and then use the addenda to piece together, oh, there's a method to the madness. Because I did at least start suspecting that there was some kind of divine vengeance going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah it would be nice if there was some commentary about how these incidents are statistically improbable, and/or some parapsychology technobabble about EMF and EVP.

Otherwise we really just have to take it on faith that these aren't merely coincidences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm mainly confused as to why this is considered anomalous. It seems like a hospital where sometimes bad things happen. My biggest question is, what made the psych ward the initial SCP? What was anomalous about it? I sort of maybe get what's going on in those addenda, but at least one doesn't seem like anything anomalous at all, even when held up against the others.

I don't want to downvote since this is otherwise well-written and has a classic feel to it. I just more or less don't get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. It's interesting, thought-out and has lots of detail, but a reader really shouldn't have to go back and forth through the article before understanding what exactly makes the hospital anomalous beyond an assortment of strange events that tangentially relate to the patient's lives in some way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Incident B
– hide block


The wounded officer was shot with a .45 ACP round and officer Dunn brought a M1911 into the hospital, when visiting Walker. The haunting mistakenly concluded that Dunn had shot Walker.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry about the comment-less change, I clicked on the wrong button before typing comment but it's just some missing or double words. Also:

+ Report b
- Report b

I don't get what you're implying with the Colt M1911.

- Report b
+ Report d
- Report d

Any connection? I understands the retribution theme but this one is too vague for me.

- Report d

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I shouldn't need 8 hints to glean anything from this. If the concept isn't conveyed without the author stepping in and explaining it, then the concept wasn't conveyed. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, the thing with incident e was accidental - I had changed around who got injured how and sent where, but hadn't updated all of the events to match. Thanks for catching that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not entirely sure how to feel about this one. It's an interesting storytelling gambit burying the common thread through cryptic hints in many incident logs. It was kind of fun puzzling it out (although I did need your hints on the end), but I can't help but feel that there's a large amount of material that is simply unnecessary bureaucrat-ese and chaff. The bits about the psychiatric ward and the burned document, for instance, might make sense as an origin story but didn't really add anything to the overall narrative. They just made it take longer to get to the story.

Also, I don't know if this is intentional, but the details of incident e seem entirely mixed up.
- Hazen, Silvera and Moore are trapped in the wreckage, but the fire dept only recovers Silvera and Moore.
- Hazen and Silvera are ambulanced to the SCP (the others are helicoptered elsewhere), but Hazen and Knotts arrive at the SCP.
Is there a narrative reason for this shuffling that I've missed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. This is so bizarre and unsettling. I love it.

On a meta-note: I really like how the first incident is just destroyed. I feel like too often data is restricted or expunged or redacted and that it's important that sometimes shit just gets burnt up by accident.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad. I like a good old-fashioned haunting and this does it quite well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very close, except

+ show block
– hide block

Mr. Dean died from carbon monoxide poisoning, not from ethylene glycol, which is what he used to poison Mr. Moyer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Initial downvote cancelled when I re-read this and worked it out. It is still very dry, so I'll need to think about it some more before I decide on an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Incident d still doesn't feel anomalous to me, but I really like the concept and how it's conveyed. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
About the last one. Was it:

+ show block
– hide block

The kidney failure was due to poisoning. The hospital, or whatever entity was causing the 'haunting', initially assumed it was his wife's doing — until his business partner visited him and tried to poison him. The entity caused the partner to be the one to suffer the effects of the poison he had handed to the patient.

item.title: About dem needles
item.content:encoded:
+ spoier
– hide block

The number of 10cc needles isn't random, people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I must admit I didn't get it, but gradually figuring out bits and coming to more-or-less understand it after receiving a brief explanation was immensely satisfying

That and all the nice little details you've put in, is making me give this one a +1.

I'm still not sure it's clear enough in-article, but obviously it's hard to tell isn't it, once you know what's going on?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a collaboration between myself and TrainMan2001.

A special thanks to Some_Potato for advice regarding medical things, specifically emergency medicine, and his help with reviewing it in the final stages of completion. Another special thanks goes to ribby97 for providing detailed feedback in the final stages of editing, allowing me to remove all the rough edges and catching the little things that I couldn't after staring at it for several hours.

In no particular order, I would like to thank all of the following people for their feedback while we were creating this SCP:

  • Decibelles
  • GreenWolf
  • DreadLindwyrm
  • salvagebar

If I missed anyone, please PM me so I can add you.

Character were generated using http://www.fakenamegenerator.com/ and are licensed under CC-BY-SA-3.0.

Note that all of the phone numbers listed except the Lake County Sheriff's Office are not real phone numbers - the exchange code (the set of three digits in the middle) is invalid, and thus those numbers will never be assigned to a subscriber. The Sheriff phone number is the actual phone number for the Lake County Sheriff non-emergency line.

If you didn't get it, or you want to confirm your theories, here are a few hints that may be able to help you. (Although honestly if you didn't get any of it the hints might just confuse you further.)

+ (hint) Incident Report 2564-b
– hide block

Indiana State Police only recently switched to using the SIG Sauer P227 .45 ACP; before they had used the Glock 17 9mm.

+ (hint) Incident Report 2564-c
– hide block

Kyle Law was an abusive parent.

+ (hint) Incident Report 2564-d
– hide block

Edinburgh Correctional Facility is a minimum security facility.

+ (hint) Incident Report 2564-d
– hide block

It is uncommon for a 28-year-old to die of a stroke.

+ (hint) Incident Report 2564-e
– hide block

The human body typically contains between 4.7 and 5 liters of blood.

+ (hint) Incident Report 2564-f
– hide block

Ethylene glycol is more commonly known as antifreeze.

+ (hint) General
– hide block

The Hospital is not always right.

If you are still confused or want to confirm your theories, I have now added a set of spoilers explaining it all on the second page of the comments.
